course.title: Learn Python
course.description: Master the world's most versatile programming language.

chapter.title: Variables & Data Types
chapter.description: Learn how to store and manipulate different types of data in Python.

lessons.title: Variables and Assignment
objectives: Learn how to assign values to variables
lessons.explanation:
In programming, a variable is essentially a container for storing data values. Unlike other programming languages, Python has no command for declaring a variable. A variable is created the moment you first assign a value to it.

Python variables are dynamically typed, meaning you don't need to specify what type of data the variable holds—Python figures it out automatically based on the value you assign.
lessons.analogy: Think of a variable as a labeled box. You can put things into the box (assignment) and whenever you read the label on the box, you are actually referring to whatever is inside it.
common_mistakes: Starting a variable name with a number: `1st_place = "John"` (This causes a SyntaxError).
Using spaces in variable names: `my name = "Alice"` (Use underscores instead: `my_name = "Alice"`).
best_practices: Use snake_case for variable names (all lowercase, words separated by underscores).
Use descriptive names. `age = 25` is much better than `x = 25`.
lessons.summary: You've learned that variables are used to store data, and they are created the moment you assign a value using the `=` operator.

exercises.statement: Create a variable named `favorite_color` and assign the string `"blue"` to it. Then, print the variable.

lessons.title: Data Types: Strings & Numbers
lessons.explanation:
Data types classify what kind of data a variable holds. Python has several built-in data types, but the most common are:

1. **Strings (`str`)**: Used for text. They are wrapped in single or double quotes.
2. **Integers (`int`)**: Whole numbers, positive or negative, without decimals.
3. **Floats (`float`)**: Numbers containing a decimal point.

You can perform math on numbers using operators like `+` (addition), `-` (subtraction), `*` (multiplication), and `/` (division).
lessons.analogy: Data types are like physical properties of objects. You can do math with numbers just like you can measure liquids, but you can't divide a word by 2, just like you can't pour a solid brick.
common_mistakes: Trying to add a string and an integer: `"Age: " + 25` will cause a TypeError. You must convert the number to a string first using `str()`.
best_practices: Always be aware of what data type your variable holds, especially when taking input from users (which is always a string by default).
lessons.summary: Strings hold text, while integers and floats hold numeric values. Python allows you to perform standard mathematical operations on numeric data types easily.

exercises.statement: Create a variable `apples` equal to 5, and `oranges` equal to 3. Add them together in a variable called `total_fruit`, and print `total_fruit`.
exercises.starter_code:
apples = 5
oranges = 3
